critic_raw:
This CNN ECG vs. logistic regression cardiac risk prediction experiment has four critical methodological issues.

1. Random stratified split on 5-year temporal cardiac data violates deployment causality. 'Partitioning will use stratified random sampling across all available years.' Cardiac risk prediction in deployment means the model is trained on historical patients and predicts outcomes for future patients. A random split will put some 2023 patients in training and 2019 patients in test — the model predicts outcomes of patients who had their events before many of its training patients did. This produces an unrealistically optimistic AUC estimate. Additionally, clinical practice and cardiac intervention thresholds change over 5 years; randomly mixing time periods conceals temporal distribution shift that a deployed model would face. Evidence: implement a temporal split (train on years 1-3, validate on year 4, test on year 5) and compare AUROC; if AUROC drops materially, the random split was masking temporal distribution shift.

2. The comparison is CNN on ECG waveforms vs. logistic regression on clinical risk factors — not CNN vs. LR on the same features. The ECG waveform contains information that does not appear in clinical risk factors (e.g., QRS morphology, T-wave changes, ST deviation). A fair comparison of model architecture would hold features constant — either both models on ECG-derived features or both on clinical features. The current design confounds feature type with model type: the CNN advantage could be entirely attributable to ECG information content, with a logistic regression on ECG-derived features (QT interval, ST slope, heart rate variability) matching or exceeding the CNN. Evidence: include a logistic regression baseline trained on classical ECG-derived features (QT interval, PR interval, ST elevation, LBBB flag, LVH voltage criteria) rather than only clinical risk factors; this is the appropriate comparison for the stated hypothesis.

3. Section 6 describes validation strategy inconsistently. The text states 'All hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation cohort (year 4, ~2,000 patients)' — implying a temporal split for validation selection — but Section 2 uses a random split for the overall data partition. If the split is random, 'year 4' is not a coherent temporal cohort; patients from year 4 appear throughout train, validation, and test by design. The validation 'year 4' description contradicts the random split description in Section 2. This inconsistency means either the split strategy is not as described or the validation description is wrong. Evidence: reconcile Sections 2 and 6; if year 4 is a temporal cohort, the overall split must be temporal, not random.

4. Preprocessing fit on training data but applied to 5-second standardized ECG windows without verifying population coverage. ECG signals are standardized to 5 seconds (1,250 samples). Z-score normalization per lead uses training-set mean and SD. If the test population includes patients with significantly different QRS durations (e.g., patients with bundle branch blocks, high degree of arrhythmia) whose ECG baseline amplitudes differ from the training population, the training-set normalization may not center their signals appropriately — leading to distribution mismatch between training and test. This is not leakage but a silent misconfiguration: the normalization is technically correct but may fail on the population of patients most relevant to the hypothesis (high-risk patients with abnormal ECG morphology). Evidence: report normalization statistics (mean amplitude, SD) stratified by positive vs. negative outcome; if positive cases have systematically different amplitudes than negative cases, the normalization may suppress or amplify the discriminative signal differentially.
all_issues_raised: Random stratified split on 5-year temporal cardiac data violates deployment causality — future patients appear in training, past patients in test
Comparison conflates feature type with model type — CNN on ECG vs. LR on clinical features does not isolate architecture from information content
Inconsistency between random split (Section 2) and year 4 temporal validation cohort (Section 6)
Z-score normalization per lead may fail silently on high-risk patients with abnormal ECG morphology — distribution mismatch between positive and negative cases
Stratified random split on 5-year ECG data ignores temporal confounding from evolving clinical practice and equipment
CNN and logistic regression receive non-overlapping information types; comparison tests information sets rather than model architectures
12-month outcome window combined with 5-year data collection creates censoring risk for late-enrolled patients; censoring handling is absent
Validation set definition as 'year 4' contradicts the stratified random split description in Section 2; internal inconsistency in split strategy
No ablation or feature attribution prevents understanding which ECG waveform features drive CNN performance advantage
Stratified random split across 5 years with 12-month outcome follow-up creates informative censoring; late-enrollment patients may have incomplete outcome ascertainment
Comparison of CNN-on-waveform vs. LR-on-clinical-features conflates modality with architecture; missing LR-on-ECG-features condition needed to isolate architecture effect
Inconsistent split design: validation is temporally stratified (year 4) but test set is randomly sampled; temporal generalization only tested at validation stage
Zero-padding of short ECGs creates detectable artifact features correlated with recording quality; CNN may learn this confound

Zero-padding of short ECGs creates detectable artifact features correlated with recording quality; CNN may learn this confound
